elements.sent-2411-004: Recently there has been a large number of works on bilinear sums with Kloosterman sums and on sums of Kloosterman sums twisted by arithmetic functions.
elements.sent-2411-005: Motivated by these, we consider several related new questions about sums of Kloosterman sums parametrised by square-free and smooth integers.
elements.sent-2411-006: For a prime $p$ and an integer $n$, we define the $s$-dimensional Kloosterman sum \begin{equation}\cK_{s, p}(n) = p^{-(s-1)/2} \sum_{\substack{x_1, \ldots, x_s=1 x_1\cdots x_s \equiv n \pmod p}}^{p-1} \ep\(x_1 +\cdots + x_s\)\end{equation} where $\ep(x) = \exp(2\pi ix/p)$.
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\cK_{s, p}(n) = p^{-(s-1)/2} \sum_{\substack{x_1, \ldots, x_s=1 x_1\cdots x_s \equiv n \pmod p}}^{p-1} \ep\(x_1 +\cdots + x_s\)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-007: The celebrated result of Deligne~\cite{Del gives the following bound: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Deligne} |\cK_{s, p}(n)| \le s.\end{equation}. See also~\cite[Section~11.11]{IwKow .}}
elements.sent-2411-008: In the classical case of $s=2$ we denote \begin{equation}\cK_{p}(n) = \cK_{2, p}(n).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-009: Recently, there has been active interest in estimating sums of Kloosterman sums either over sequences of parameters $n$ of arithmetic interest, or twisted by arithmetic functions, such as, \begin{equation}\label{eq:Sums} P_{s, p}(L) = \sum_{\substack{\ell \le L \ell~\text{prime}}} \cK_{s, p}(\ell) \quad \text{and} \quad M_{s, p}(f;N) = \sum_{n \le N} f(n)\cK_{s, p}(n)\end{equation} with some multiplicative function $f(n)$ such as the M\"obius function $f(n) = \mu(n)$ or the divisor function $f(n) = \tau(n)$.
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Sums} P_{s, p}(L) = \sum_{\substack{\ell \le L \ell~prime}} \cK_{s, p}(\ell) \quad and \quad M_{s, p}(f;N) = \sum_{n \le N} f(n)\cK_{s, p}(n)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-010: See, for example,~\cite{BFKMM2, FKM1, KoSh, LSZ .}
elements.sent-2411-011: These results largely rely on recent progress on bounds of bilinear Type~I and Type~II sums with Kloosterman sums; see~\cite{BagShp, BFKMM1, BFKMM2, FKM1, KSWX,KMS1,KMS2, Shp, ShpZha .}
elements.sent-2411-012: In particular,~\cite[Theorem~1.5]{FKM1 implies power-saving bounds on the sums $P_{s, p (L)$ given by~\eqref{eq:Sums with $L \ge p^{3/4+\varepsilon}$ for an arbitrary fixed $\varepsilon > 0$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: These bounds were subsequently improved in~\cite[Theorem~1.8]{BFKMM2 in the case of $s=2$ and for the same range of $L$.}
elements.sent-2411-014: For the sums $M_{s, p (f; N)$ given by~\eqref{eq:Sums ,~\cite[Theorem~1.7]{FKM1 gives power-saving bounds on $M_{s, p (\mu; N)$ when $f(n) = \mu(n)$, provided that $N\ge p^{3/4+\varepsilon}$ for an arbitrary fixed $\varepsilon > 0$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Similar bounds are given by~\cite[Corollary~1.4]{KMS2 on $M_{s, p (\tau; N)$ when $f(n) = \tau(n)$, provided that $N\ge p^{2/3+\varepsilon}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: These thresholds have both been reduced to $N\ge p^{1/2+\varepsilon}$ in~\cite{KoSh , however with a logarithmic saving instead of a power saving in the bound.}
elements.subsection-2411-002: Sums of Kloosterman sums over square-free numbers
elements.sent-2411-017: In this paper we first consider sums of Kloosterman sums over square-free numbers (that is, over numbers which are not divisible by the square of a prime): \begin{equation}Q_{s, p}(N) = \sum_{n \le N} |\mu(n)| \cK_{s, p}(n) = \sum_{\substack{n \le N n~square-free}} \cK_{s, p}(n).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}Q_{s, p}(N) = \sum_{n \le N} |\mu(n)| \cK_{s, p}(n) = \sum_{\substack{n \le N n~square-free}} \cK_{s, p}(n).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-018: Note that we have the trivial bound \begin{equation}\label{eq:Triv 1} Q_{s, p}(N) \ll N\end{equation} implied by~\eqref{eq:Deligne .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Our goal is twofold: to obtain a nontrivial bound (possibly with a power saving) for $N$ as small as possible and to have a bound as good as possible for any given $N$.
elements.sent-2411-020: Our main result in this direction is the following bound.
elements.sent-2411-021: For any positive integer $s \ge 2$ and even positive integer $\ell$, if $p^{1/2+2/\ell \le N \le p$ then \begin{equation}|Q_{s, p}(N)| \le N^{1/2} p^{1/4} \(\frac{p^{1/2+2/\ell}}{N}\)^{\tfrac{1}{2(4\ell-3)}} p^{o(1)}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Our approach works for $N > p$ as well.
elements.sent-2411-023: However, the optimisation of our argument becomes more cluttered in such generality.
elements.sent-2411-024: Since we are mostly interested in short sums and in order to exhibit our ideas in tje simplest possible form, we focus on the case $N \le p$.
elements.sent-2411-025: Theorem~\ref{thm:SquareFree any s is proved in Section~\ref{sec:SquareFree .}}
elements.sent-2411-026: It in particular implies that if $p^{1/2+\varepsilon \le N \le p$ for an arbitrary fixed $\varepsilon$, then \begin{equation}\label{eq:Triv 2} Q_{s, p}(N) \ll N^{1/2} p^{1/4+o(1)}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-027: In fact, the bound~\eqref{eq:Triv 2 can be quickly obtained by combining~\eqref{eq: T-PV and~\eqref{eq: T-Deligne in our proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:SquareFree any s .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Compared to~\eqref{eq:Triv 2 , we are able to obtain additional savings in Theorem~\ref{thm:SquareFree any s by studying a certain type-I sum in Lemma~\ref{lem: Type I any s .}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: On the other hand, both the bound in Theorem~\ref{thm:SquareFree any s and~\eqref{eq:Triv 2 beat the trivial bound~\eqref{eq:Triv 1 in the range $p^{1/2+\eps \le N \le p$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: For a specific value of $N$, one can optimize the bound in Theorem~\ref{thm:SquareFree any s by making the choice of $\ell$ that minimizes the term \begin{equation}\(\frac{p^{1/2+2/\ell}}{N}\)^{\tfrac{1}{2(4\ell-3)}}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-031: For example, if $N=p$ then by choosing $\ell = 8$ we get \begin{equation}Q_{s,p}(p) \ll p^{3/4-1/232+o(1)}.\end{equation}.
elements.subsection-2411-003: Sums of Kloosterman sums over smooth numbers
elements.sent-2411-032: Next, we recall that an integer $n$ is called $y$-smooth if $P(n) \le y$, where $P(n)$ denotes the largest prime divisor of an integer $n\ge 1$.
elements.sent-2411-033: See~\cite{Granv, HilTen for backgrounds and classical estimates on smooth numbers.}
elements.sent-2411-034: For $N \ge y \ge 2$, we denote by $\cS(N,y)$ the set of $y$-smooth positive integers $n \le N$ and, as usual, we denote $\Psi(N,y) = \# \cS(N,y)$.
elements.sent-2411-035: We consider the sum \begin{equation}R_{s.p}(N,y) = \sum_{n \in \cS(N,y)} \cK_{s, p}(n) ,\end{equation} for which we have, in analogy to~\eqref{eq:Triv 1 , the trivial bound \begin{equation}R_{s, p}(N,y) \ll \Psi(N,y) \le N.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-036: As in the case of squarefree numbers, our goal is to obtain nontrivial bounds for $N$ as small as possible and, at the same time, to have a bound as good as possible for any given $N$ and $y$.
elements.sent-2411-037: Additionally, we are also interested in obtaining nontrivial bounds for $y$ as small as possible.
elements.sent-2411-038: Before formulating our main result in this direction, we recall some well-known estimates in the theory of smooth numbers.
elements.sent-2411-039: Let $\alpha(N,y)$ be the {\it saddle point\/ corresponding to the $y$-smooth numbers up to $N$ as discussed in~\cite{Harp, HilTen .}}
elements.sent-2411-040: In particular, $\alpha(N,y)$ satisfies \begin{equation}\alpha(N,y) = (1+o(1))\frac{\log(1+y/\log N)}{\log y}\end{equation} provided that $y \leq N$ and $y \to \infty$; see~\cite[Theorem~2]{HilTen .}
elements.sent-2411-041: In particular, if $\log y/\log \log N \to \infty$ then $\alpha(N,y) \to 1$, and if $y = (\log N)^K$ for some $K \geq 1$ then $\alpha(N,y) = 1-1/K + o(1)$.
elements.sent-2411-042: We have \begin{equation}\label{eq: Psi N-alpha} \Psi(N,y) = N^{\alpha(N,y) + o(1)};\end{equation} see, for example,~\cite[Section~2]{Harp or~\cite[Theorem~1]{HilTen .}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Let $p$ be prime, $N \geq p^{1/2}$, and $y \geq \log N$.
elements.sent-2411-044: Then \begin{equation}|R_{s,p}(N,y)| \le \Psi(N,y) y^{1/2} p^{\beta} N^{-\gamma + o(1)} ,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\beta = \frac{1}{4\(1+\alpha(N,y)\)} \mand \gamma= \frac{\alpha(N,y)^2}{2\(1+\alpha(N,y)\)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-045: Some remarks on the bound are in order.
elements.sent-2411-046: If $y = N^{o(1)}$ then $y^{1/2}$ can be dropped from the bound and we have \begin{equation}R_{s,p}(N,y) \ll \Psi(N,y) p^{\beta} N^{-\gamma+o(1)},\end{equation} which is non-trivial when $N \ge p^{\beta/\gamma+ \eps = p^{1/(2\alpha(N,y)^2) + \eps}$ for arbitrary fixed $\eps > 0$.}
elements.sent-2411-047: In the special case $N=p$, we have a nontrivial bound for $R_{s,p (p,y)$, provided that $\alpha(N,y) > 1/\sqrt{2}$, or $y \geq (\log N)^{2+\sqrt{2 +\eps}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-048: On the other hand, if $\log y/\log\log N\rightarrow \infty$ then $\alpha(N,y) = 1 + o(1)$, and thus Theorem~\ref{thm:smooth-Kloosterman yields \begin{equation}R_{s,p}(N,y) \ll \Psi(N,y) y^{1/2} p^{1/8} N^{-1/4+o(1)},\end{equation} which is non-trivial when $N > y^2 p^{1/2+\eps}$.}
elements.sent-2411-049: The rest of the paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-050: In Section~\ref{sec:typeI we state some preliminary lemmas including a type-I estimate which is curicial in our treatment of the square-free numbers.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Then in Sections~\ref{sec:SquareFree and~\ref{sec:smooth , we prove Theorems~\ref{thm:SquareFree any s and~\ref{thm:smooth-Kloosterman , respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: We use the standard notations $U\ll V$ and $V \gg U$ as equivalent to the statement $|U|\leq c V$, for some constant $c> 0$, which throughout this paper may depend only on the integer parameters $\ell$ and $s$.
elements.sent-2411-053: For a finite set $\cS$ we use $\# \cS$ to denote its cardinality.
elements.sent-2411-054: The variables of summation $d$, $k$, $m$ and $n$ are always positive integers.
elements.sent-2411-055: We also follow the convention that fractions of the shape $1/ab$ mean $1/(ab)$ (that than $b/a$ as their formal interpretation requires).
elements.sent-2411-056: The letter $p$ always denotes a prime number, and we use $\F_p$ to denote the finite field of $p$ elements.
elements.sent-2411-057: We denote by $p(\ell)$ and $P(\ell)$, the smallest and the largest prime factors of an integer $\ell\ne 0$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-058: We adopt the convention that $p(1) = P(1) = +\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-059: Finally, we write $\sum_{k\le K}$ to denote the summation over positive integer $k \le K$.
elements.subsection-2411-005: Preliminary bounds on sums of Kloosterman sums
elements.sent-2411-060: The following result is a special case of~\cite[Corollary~1.6]{FKM2 .}
elements.sent-2411-061: Uniformly over $h \in \Z$, and $d \in \F_p\setminus\{0,1\}$, we have \begin{equation}\sum_{x \in\F_p} \cK_{s, p}\(x\) \ep(hx)\ll p^{1/2}, \sum_{x \in\F_p} \cK_{s, p}\(x\) \cK_{s, p}\(d x\) \ep(hx) \ll p^{1/2} .\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-019: \begin{equation}\sum_{x \in\F_p} \cK_{s, p}\(x\) \ep(hx)\ll p^{1/2}, \sum_{x \in\F_p} \cK_{s, p}\(x\) \cK_{s, p}\(d x\) \ep(hx) \ll p^{1/2} .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-062: The standard completion technique (see~\cite[Section~12.2]{IwKow ) shows that Lemma~\ref{lem:FKM implies the following bound on incomplete sums.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: For any $K \le p$, uniformly over $d, e \in \F_p$ satisfying $d \ne 0$ and $e/d \ne 0, 1$, we have \begin{equation}\sum_{n \le K} \cK_{s, p}(dn) \ll p^{1/2} \log p, \sum_{n \le K} \cK_{s, p}(dn) \cK_{s, p}(en) \ll p^{1/2} \log p.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-020: \begin{equation}\sum_{n \le K} \cK_{s, p}(dn) \ll p^{1/2} \log p, \sum_{n \le K} \cK_{s, p}(dn) \cK_{s, p}(en) \ll p^{1/2} \log p.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-064: We now record a bound on a variant of Type-I sums of Kloosterman sums.
elements.sent-2411-065: Namely, instead of Type-I sums with $\cK_{s,p (mn)$ we consider sums with $\cK_{s,p (m^{r n)$ where $r$ is an arbitrary integer (if $r$ is negative we consider the argument of the corresponding Kloosterman sum modulo $p$).}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: This is obtained by a slight extension of the argument of~\cite{KMS2 .}
elements.sent-2411-067: See also~\cite{BaSh,BFKMM1,FKM1,KSWX,KMS1,KMS2 for several other bounds on related sums.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Fix an integer $r \neq 0$ and an even integer $\ell \ge 2$.
elements.sent-2411-069: Let $D, N \le p$ be positive integers with $N > 2p^{1/\ell}$.
elements.sent-2411-070: For each $d \le D$ let $\cN_d \subset [1,N]$ be an interval.
elements.sent-2411-071: Then for any complex weights \begin{equation}\alpha=\{\alpha_d\}_{d \le D}\end{equation} with $\alpha_d \ll 1$, we have \begin{equation}\sum_{d\le D} \sum_{n\in \cN_d} \alpha_d \cK_{s,p}(d^r n) \ll DN\(N^{-1} + \frac{p^{1+1/\ell}}{DN^2}\)^{1/(2\ell)} p^{o(1)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-072: We follow the proof of~\cite[Theorem~4.3]{KMS2 , which is conveniently summarised in~\cite[Section~4.3]{BaSh and also extended to sums when the non-smooth variable runs through an arbitrary set.. Let \begin{equation}S = \sum_{d\le D} \sum_{n\in \cN_d} \alpha_d \cK_{s,p}(d^r n).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-073: Let $A$ and $B$ be integer parameters for which \begin{equation}\label{eq:AB N} 2AB\le N\end{equation} to be chosen later.
elements.sent-2411-074: By introducing averages over $a \sim A$ and $b \sim B$ (where $a \sim A$ denotes the dyadic range $A \leq a < 2A$ and similarly for $b \sim B$) and replacing $n$ by $n+ab$, we have \begin{equation}\begin{split} S = \frac{1}{AB} \sum_{a \sim A} \sum_{b \sim B} \sum_{d \leq D} \alpha_d \sum_{\substack{n\in \Z n+ab \in \cN_d}} \cK_{s,p}(d^r(n+ab)) = \frac{1}{AB} \sum_{a \sim A} \sum_{d \leq D} \alpha_d \sum_{n\in \Z} \sum_{\substack{b \sim B n+ab \in \cN_d}} \cK_{s,p}(d^r(n+ab)). \end{split}\end{equation} Since the range for the inner sum over $b$ is an interval, by the completing technique (see~\cite[Section~12.2]{IwKow ), we have \begin{equation}S \ll \frac{\log p}{AB} \sum_{a \sim A} \sum_{d \leq D} \sum_{n=-N}^N \left|\sum_{b \sim B}\cK_{s,p}(d^r(n+ab)) \e(bt) \right|\end{equation} for some $t \in \R$, where $ \e(z) = \exp(2\pi i z)$.}
elements.formula-2411-025: \begin{equation} S = \frac{1}{AB} \sum_{a \sim A} \sum_{b \sim B} \sum_{d \leq D} \alpha_d \sum_{\substack{n\in \Z n+ab \in \cN_d}} \cK_{s,p}(d^r(n+ab)) = \frac{1}{AB} \sum_{a \sim A} \sum_{d \leq D} \alpha_d \sum_{n\in \Z} \sum_{\substack{b \sim B n+ab \in \cN_d}} \cK_{s,p}(d^r(n+ab)). \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-075: By making a change of variables $u = d^ra$ and $v = \overline{a n$, we obtain \begin{equation}S \ll \frac{\log p}{AB} \sum_{u, v \in \F_p} \nu(u,v) \left|\sum_{b \sim B} \cK_{s,p}(u(v+b)) \e(bt) \right|,\end{equation} where $\nu(u,v)$ is the number of triples $(a,m,n)$ with $a \sim A$, $m \in \{d^r:~1\leq d \leq D\}$ and $n \in [-N, N]$ such that \begin{equation}u \equiv \overline{a}n \pmod{p} \mand v \equiv ma\pmod{p}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-076: Following the steps in~\cite[Section~4.3]{BaSh leading to~\cite[Equations~(4.9), (4.10), and~(4.11)]{BaSh and defining $\cM = \{d^r:~1\leq d \leq D\}$, we obtain \begin{equation}\label{eq:FKM-bound} S^{2\ell} \ll A^{-2}B^{-2\ell} D^{2\ell-2} N^{2\ell-1} \(B^\ell p^2 + B^{2\ell} p\) J(2A,\cM) p^{o(1)},\end{equation} where $J(H,\cM)$ denotes the number of solutions to the congruence \begin{equation}\label{eq:Congr} x k\equiv ym\bmod p\end{equation} for which $x,y\in[1,H]$ and $k,m \in \cM$.}}
elements.formula-2411-029: \begin{equation}\label{eq:FKM-bound} S^{2\ell} \ll A^{-2}B^{-2\ell} D^{2\ell-2} N^{2\ell-1} \(B^\ell p^2 + B^{2\ell} p\) J(2A,\cM) p^{o(1)},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-077: Taking $B = \lfloor p^{1/\ell}\rfloor$, we see that~\eqref{eq:FKM-bound simplifies as \begin{equation}\label{eq:FKM-bound-simpl} S^{2\ell} \ll A^{-2} D^{2\ell-2} N^{2\ell-1} J(2A,\cM) p^{1+o(1)}.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-031: \begin{equation}\label{eq:FKM-bound-simpl} S^{2\ell} \ll A^{-2} D^{2\ell-2} N^{2\ell-1} J(2A,\cM) p^{1+o(1)}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-078: In the setting of the proof of~\cite[Theorem~4.3]{KMS2 , the condition \begin{equation}\label{eq:FKM-cond} 2A\max_{m\in \cM} m < p\end{equation} was satisfied, which allows us to replace~\eqref{eq:Congr with an equation $x k = ym$ in integers.}}
elements.sent-2411-079: Then, using the classical bound on the divisor function (see~\cite[Equation~(1.81)]{IwKow ), it is shown in~\cite{KMS2 that, under the condition~\eqref{eq:FKM-cond we have $J(2A,\cM) \le \(A\# \cM\)^{1+ o(1)}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: However,~\eqref{eq:FKM-cond is too restrictive for our purpose, so we instead use a result of Ayyad, Cochrane and Zheng~\cite[Theorem~2]{ACZ which, similarly as in the proof of~\cite[Theorem~4.1]{CSZ , leads to the bound \begin{equation}\label{eq:J Bound} J(2A,\cM) \ll A^2D^2/p + (AD)^{1+ o(1)}.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-081: Substituting~\eqref{eq:J Bound in~\eqref{eq:FKM-bound-simpl , we obtain \begin{equation}S^{2\ell} \ll \(D^{2\ell} N^{2\ell-1} + A^{-1} D^{2\ell-1} N^{2\ell-1}p\) p^{o(1)}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-082: Since $N > 2p^{1/\ell \ge 2B$, we may choose \begin{equation}A=\Big\lfloor \frac{N}{2B}\Big\rfloor \gg N p^{-1/\ell}\end{equation} which guarantees that the condition~\eqref{eq:AB N is met.}}
elements.sent-2411-083: We obtain the stated bound after simple calculations.
elements.sent-2411-084: Using inclusion-exclusion, we can write \begin{equation}Q_{s, p}(N) = \sum_{d \le N^{1/2} }\mu(d)\sum_{n\le N/d^2} \cK_{s, p} (d^2n).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-085: Next, we split the sum $Q_{s, p (N)$ into dyadic intervals with respect to some parameter $D \ge 1$ to get $O(\log N)$ sums of the type \begin{equation}S(D,N)=\sum_{d\sim D}\mu(d)\sum_{n\le N/d^2} \cK_{s, p} (d^2n)\end{equation} for some $D \le N^{1/2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-086: By Lemma~\ref{lem:FKM Incompl we have \begin{equation}\label{eq: T-PV} S(D,N)\ll Dp^{1/2}\log p.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-087: By the Deligne bound~\eqref{eq:Deligne we have \begin{equation}\label{eq: T-Deligne} S(D,N) \ll DN/D^2 = N/D.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-088: On the other hand, for any fixed even integer $\ell>0$, by Lemma~\ref{lem: Type I any s we have \begin{equation}S(D,N) \ll DK\(K^{-1} + \frac{p^{1+1/\ell}}{DK^2}\)^{1/(2\ell)} p^{o(1)},\end{equation} where $K=N/D^2$, provided that $K > 2p^{1/\ell}$.}
elements.sent-2411-089: Note that \begin{equation}DK = N/D \le N \le p \le p^{1+1/\ell},\end{equation} and thus \begin{equation}K^{-1} \le \frac{p^{1+1/\ell}}{DK^2}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-090: It follows that if $K > 2p^{1/\ell}$ then \begin{equation}\label{eq:BilinBound s} \begin{split} S(D,N) \ll DK\(\frac{p^{1+1/\ell}}{DK^2}\)^{1/(2\ell)}p^{o(1)} = \frac{N}{D}\left( \frac{D^3p^{1+1/\ell}}{N^2} \right)^{1/(2\ell)}p^{o(1)}. \end{split}\end{equation} Using the bounds~\eqref{eq: T-PV or~\eqref{eq:BilinBound s for $K > 2p^{1/\ell}$ and the bound~\eqref{eq: T-Deligne for $K \leq 2p^{1/\ell}$, we arrive at \begin{equation}\label{eq: T-overall} S(D,N) \ll \min\left\{f_1(D), f_2(D)\right\} p^{o(1)} + N^{1/2} p^{1/2\ell},\end{equation} where \begin{equation}f_1(D) = Dp^{1/2} \mand f_2(D) = \frac{N}{D}\left( \frac{D^3p^{1+1/\ell}}{N^2} \right)^{1/(2\ell)}.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.formula-2411-044: \begin{equation}\label{eq: T-overall} S(D,N) \ll \min\left\{f_1(D), f_2(D)\right\} p^{o(1)} + N^{1/2} p^{1/2\ell},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-091: Choose parameter \begin{equation}D_0 = \(\frac{N^{2\ell-2}}{p^{\ell-1-1/\ell}}\)^{\tfrac{1}{4\ell-3}} = N^{1/2}p^{-1/4} \(\frac{p^{1/2+2/\ell}}{N}\)^{\tfrac{1}{2(4\ell-3)}}\end{equation} such that $f_1(D_0) = f_2(D_0)$.
elements.sent-2411-092: Clearly $1 \le D_0 \le N^{1/2}$ since $N \geq p^{1/2 + 2/\ell}$ by assumption.. Hence \begin{equation}\min\left\{f_1(D), f_2(D)\right\} \le f_1(D_0) \le N^{1/2}p^{1/4} \(\frac{p^{1/2+2/\ell}}{N}\)^{\tfrac{1}{2(4\ell-3)}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-093: It can be easily verified that \begin{equation}p^{\frac{1}{2\ell}} \le p^{1/4} \(\frac{p^{1/2+2/\ell}}{p}\)^{\tfrac{1}{2(4\ell-3)}} \le p^{1/4} \(\frac{p^{1/2+2/\ell}}{N}\)^{\tfrac{1}{2(4\ell-3)}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-094: Hence the second term on the right-hand side of~\eqref{eq: T-overall is dominated by the first term and we have \begin{equation}S(D,N) \ll N^{1/2}p^{1/4} \(\frac{p^{1/2+2/\ell}}{N}\)^{\tfrac{1}{2(4\ell-3)}} p^{o(1)}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-096: Let $L_0 \in [1, N]$ be a parameter to be chosen later.
elements.sent-2411-097: Observe that any $y$-smooth integer in $(L_0, N]$ can be uniquely factored as $n = \ell m$ such that \begin{equation}\ell \in (L_0, yL_0], \qquad \frac{\ell}{P(\ell)} \leq L_0,\qquad p(m) \geq P(\ell),\end{equation} where $P(\ell)$ denotes the largest prime factor of $\ell$ and $p(m)$ denotes the smallest prime factor of $m$.
elements.sent-2411-098: Indeed, this factorization can be obtained by writing $n = p_1p_2\cdots p_k$ with primes $p_1 \le \cdots \le p_k$ and by setting $\ell = p_1p_2\cdots p_r$ where $r$ is the smallest positive integer such that $p_1p_2\cdots p_r > L_0$.
elements.sent-2411-099: Thus we have \begin{equation}R_{s,p}(N,y) = \sum_{\substack{L_0 < \ell \leq P(\ell) L_0 \ell \in S(y)}} \, \sum_{\substack{m \in S(N/\ell, y) p(m) \geq P(\ell)}} \cK_{s,p}(\ell m) + O(L_0).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-051: \begin{equation}R_{s,p}(N,y) = \sum_{\substack{L_0 < \ell \leq P(\ell) L_0 \ell \in S(y)}} \, \sum_{\substack{m \in S(N/\ell, y) p(m) \geq P(\ell)}} \cK_{s,p}(\ell m) + O(L_0).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-100: After dyadic partition of the range for $\ell$, we see that there is $L \in (L_0, yL_0]$ such that \begin{equation}\label{eq: R and U} R_{s,p}(N,y) \ll U \log N + L_0,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}U= \sum_{\substack{L < \ell \leq \min\left\{P(\ell)L_0, 2L\right\} \ell \in S(y)}} \, \sum_{\substack{m \in S(N/\ell, y) p(m) \geq P(\ell)}} \cK_{s,p}(\ell m).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-052: \begin{equation}\label{eq: R and U} R_{s,p}(N,y) \ll U \log N + L_0,\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-053: \begin{equation}U= \sum_{\substack{L < \ell \leq \min\left\{P(\ell)L_0, 2L\right\} \ell \in S(y)}} \, \sum_{\substack{m \in S(N/\ell, y) p(m) \geq P(\ell)}} \cK_{s,p}(\ell m).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-101: We now employ the completing technique as in~\cite[Section~12.2]{IwKow again.}
elements.sent-2411-102: That is, first we write \begin{equation}U = \sum_{\substack{L < \ell \leq \min\left\{P(\ell)L_0, 2L\right\} \ell \in S(y)}} \, \sum_{\substack{m \in S(N/L, y) p(m) \geq P(\ell)}} \cK_{s,p}(\ell m) \qquad \qquad \qquad\qquad\qquad \frac{1}{N} \sum_{ 1 \le k \le N/\ell} \sum_{a=1}^N \e\(a (m-k)/N\).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-054: \begin{equation}U = \sum_{\substack{L < \ell \leq \min\left\{P(\ell)L_0, 2L\right\} \ell \in S(y)}} \, \sum_{\substack{m \in S(N/L, y) p(m) \geq P(\ell)}} \cK_{s,p}(\ell m) \qquad \qquad \qquad\qquad\qquad \frac{1}{N} \sum_{ 1 \le k \le N/\ell} \sum_{a=1}^N \e\(a (m-k)/N\).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-103: After changing the order of summation and using~\cite[Equation~(8.6)]{IwKow (similarly to how we have done in Section~\ref{sec:SquareFree ), we derive \begin{equation}U \ll \sum_{\substack{\ell \sim L \ell \in S(y)}} \left| \sum_{\substack{m \in S(N/L, y) p(m) \geq P(\ell)}} \cK_{s,p}(\ell m) \e(\eta m) \right| \log N\end{equation} for some real $\eta \in \R$, where, as before, $ \e(z) = \exp(2\pi i z)$.}}
elements.formula-2411-055: \begin{equation}U \ll \sum_{\substack{\ell \sim L \ell \in S(y)}} \left| \sum_{\substack{m \in S(N/L, y) p(m) \geq P(\ell)}} \cK_{s,p}(\ell m) \e(\eta m) \right| \log N\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-104: By the Cauchy--Schwarz inequality, we have \begin{equation}U^2 \ll (\log N)^2 \Psi(L, y) \sum_{\substack{\ell \sim L \ell \in S(y)}} \left| \sum_{\substack{m \in S(N/L, y) p(m) \geq P(\ell)}} \cK_{s,p}(\ell m) \e(\eta m) \right|^2.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-056: \begin{equation}U^2 \ll (\log N)^2 \Psi(L, y) \sum_{\substack{\ell \sim L \ell \in S(y)}} \left| \sum_{\substack{m \in S(N/L, y) p(m) \geq P(\ell)}} \cK_{s,p}(\ell m) \e(\eta m) \right|^2.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-105: Writing $q = P(\ell)$ and replacing $\ell$ by $q\ell$, we have \begin{equation}U^2 \ll (\log N)^2 \Psi(L, y) \sum_{q \leq y} \sum_{\ell \sim L/q} \left| \sum_{\substack{m \in S(N/L, y) p(m) \geq q}} \cK_{s,p}(q \ell m) \e(\eta m) \right|^2,\end{equation} where we have dropped the primality condition on $q$.
elements.formula-2411-057: \begin{equation}U^2 \ll (\log N)^2 \Psi(L, y) \sum_{q \leq y} \sum_{\ell \sim L/q} \left| \sum_{\substack{m \in S(N/L, y) p(m) \geq q}} \cK_{s,p}(q \ell m) \e(\eta m) \right|^2,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-106: Expanding the square, we get \begin{equation}\label{eq:smooth1} \begin{split} U^2 \ll (\log N)^2 \Psi(L,y) \times \sum_{q \leq y} \sum_{m_1,m_2 \in S(N/L,y)} \left| \sum_{\ell \sim L/q} \cK_{s,p}(q\ell m_1) \overline{\cK_{s,p}(q\ell m_2)} \right|. \end{split}\end{equation} The contribution $Y_1$ to the right-hand side of~\eqref{eq:smooth1 from the diagonal terms with $m_1=m_2$ is \begin{equation}Y_1 \ll (\log N)^2 \Psi(L,y) \sum_{q \leq y} \Psi(N/L, y) \frac{L}{q} \ll L N^{-\alpha} \Psi(N,y)^2 (\log N)^3,\end{equation} where $\alpha= \alpha(N,y)$ and we have also used the standard bounds (see~\cite[Section~2]{Harp ) \begin{equation}\Psi(L, y) \ll \left(\frac{L}{N}\right)^{\alpha}\Psi(N,y),\quad\Psi(N/L,y) \ll \frac{1}{L^{\alpha}} \Psi(N,y).\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-058: \begin{equation}\label{eq:smooth1}  U^2 \ll (\log N)^2 \Psi(L,y) \times \sum_{q \leq y} \sum_{m_1,m_2 \in S(N/L,y)} \left| \sum_{\ell \sim L/q} \cK_{s,p}(q\ell m_1) \overline{\cK_{s,p}(q\ell m_2)} \right|. \end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-059: \begin{equation}Y_1 \ll (\log N)^2 \Psi(L,y) \sum_{q \leq y} \Psi(N/L, y) \frac{L}{q} \ll L N^{-\alpha} \Psi(N,y)^2 (\log N)^3,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-107: To estimate the contribution $Y_2$ to the right-hand side of~\eqref{eq:smooth1 from the non-diagonal terms with $m_1 \neq m_2$, we observe that Lemma~\ref{lem:FKM Incompl implies that \begin{equation}\left| \sum_{\ell \sim L/q} K_p(q\ell m_1) \overline{K_p(q\ell m_2)} \right| \ll p^{1/2} \log p\end{equation} when $m_1 \neq m_2$.. Hence \begin{equation}Y_2 \ll (\log N)^2 \Psi(L,y)\cdot y \Psi(N/L, y)^2 p^{1/2}\log p \ll y p^{1/2} L^{-\alpha} \Psi(N,y)^2 N^{o(1)}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-061: \begin{equation}\left| \sum_{\ell \sim L/q} K_p(q\ell m_1) \overline{K_p(q\ell m_2)} \right| \ll p^{1/2} \log p\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-062: \begin{equation}Y_2 \ll (\log N)^2 \Psi(L,y)\cdot y \Psi(N/L, y)^2 p^{1/2}\log p \ll y p^{1/2} L^{-\alpha} \Psi(N,y)^2 N^{o(1)}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-108: Overall, with $\alpha = \alpha(N,y)$, we then have \begin{equation}U^2 \ll Y_1 + Y_2 \le (LN^{-\alpha} + yp^{1/2}L^{-\alpha}) \Psi(N,y)^2 N^{o(1)} \le y\(L_0N^{-\alpha} + p^{1/2}L_0^{-\alpha}\) \Psi(N,y)^2 N^{o(1)}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-063: \begin{equation}U^2 \ll Y_1 + Y_2 \le (LN^{-\alpha} + yp^{1/2}L^{-\alpha}) \Psi(N,y)^2 N^{o(1)} \le y\(L_0N^{-\alpha} + p^{1/2}L_0^{-\alpha}\) \Psi(N,y)^2 N^{o(1)}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-109: Choosing $L_0 = p^{1/2(1+\alpha) N^{\alpha/(1+\alpha)}$ to balance the two terms depending on $L_0$, we conclude that \begin{equation}U \le y^{1/2} p^{\frac{1}{4(1+\alpha)}} N^{-\frac{\alpha^2}{2(1+\alpha)}} \Psi(N,y) N^{o(1)}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-110: Hence we see from~\eqref{eq: R and U that \begin{equation}R_{s,p}(N,y) \ll \Psi(N,y) y^{1/2} p^{\frac{1}{4(1+\alpha)}} N^{-\frac{\alpha^2}{2(1+\alpha)}+o(1)} + p^{1/2(1+\alpha)} N^{\alpha/(1+\alpha)} = \Psi(N,y) y^{1/2} p^{\beta} N^{-\gamma + o(1)} + p^{2\beta} N^{\alpha -2 \gamma}.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-065: \begin{equation}R_{s,p}(N,y) \ll \Psi(N,y) y^{1/2} p^{\frac{1}{4(1+\alpha)}} N^{-\frac{\alpha^2}{2(1+\alpha)}+o(1)} + p^{1/2(1+\alpha)} N^{\alpha/(1+\alpha)} = \Psi(N,y) y^{1/2} p^{\beta} N^{-\gamma + o(1)} + p^{2\beta} N^{\alpha -2 \gamma}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-111: We may assume that $p^{\beta \le N^{\gamma}$ since otherwise the claimed bound is trivial.}
elements.sent-2411-112: Using~\eqref{eq: Psi N-alpha , we derive \begin{equation}p^{2\beta} N^{\alpha -2 \gamma} \le p^{\beta} N^{\alpha -\gamma} = \Psi(N,y)p^{\beta} N^{-\gamma + o(1)},\end{equation} and the result follows.}
